splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or whether actuaries may select assumptions after that date. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be determined as of or before the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability about potential withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current data even if it postdates the end of the prior plan year.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the supervision period beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond each year and raises questions about the limits of judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Supervised release is tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, pausing the clock so that the period of supervision is extended by the duration of the absence.

positions.label: Supervised release is not tolled
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release continues to run even when a supervisee absconds, because there is no statutory authority for tolling and courts may not judicially create such a doctrine to extend punishment.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits have split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face a categorical bar under § 2244(b)(1) against raising any claim they previously presented in a successive § 2255 motion, in addition to the separate requirements in § 2255(h). The resolution determines whether federal prisoners face a stricter gatekeeping requirement than Congress intended for successive collateral attacks.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1) on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, creating an additional hurdle beyond § 2255(h).

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions; they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have Article III standing to bring pre-election challenges to state voting rules, including ballot-counting deadlines. The resolution affects how easily pre-election challenges can be brought to voting procedures nationwide and whether such disputes are funneled into post-election litigation.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate has Article III standing to challenge election rules that allegedly affect the validity or counting of ballots in their race, recognizing competitive injury as sufficient.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's generalized competitive interest is insufficient to establish Article III standing to challenge state ballot-counting procedures before an election.

circuits.name: Third Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bognet v. Secretary of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of the right to make and enforce contracts?
splits.description: Circuits have taken different approaches to the standard for § 1981 claims in hospitality and service contexts, particularly regarding what constitutes interference with the right to make and enforce contracts and the available remedies. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach against other circuits' frameworks.

positions.label: Narrower standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 claims require a showing of interference with an actual contractual relationship or attempted contract, applying a more restrictive view of what constitutes actionable discrimination.

positions.label: Broader standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 covers a broader range of discriminatory conduct in business establishments, including degraded service or hostile treatment that impairs the enjoyment of contracted services.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over deference to agency or Sentencing Commission interpretations when construing ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether lenity trumps Auer/Kisor deference to the Sentencing Commission's commentary when interpreting ambiguous Guidelines provisions that increase criminal penalties. At least three circuits hold lenity takes priority, while others (including the Third Circuit panel in United States v. Chandler) have held that agency deference can override lenity. This split has deepened after Loper Bright overruled Chevron.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity prevails over deference to agency or Commission interpretations when construing ambiguous criminal or sentencing provisions, preventing agencies from using delegated power to expand criminal liability.

positions.label: Deference can override lenity
positions.summary: Holds that deference to the Sentencing Commission's authoritative interpretations of ambiguous Guidelines provisions takes precedence over the rule of lenity, even in criminal sentencing contexts.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy, based on Application Note 1's expansion of the guideline text.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2 — which adds conspiracy, aiding and abetting, and attempt to the definition of 'controlled substance offense' — is a permissible interpretation entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor, or whether the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses. This split affects career-offender designations for defendants with prior conspiracy convictions.

positions.label: Application Note 1 is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1's inclusion of inchoate offenses is a reasonable interpretation of the ambiguous guideline text and is entitled to controlling weight under Stinson, as reaffirmed by Kisor.

positions.label: Guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that the text of § 4B1.2(b) is unambiguous in not including inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline beyond its text, so it is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify) or disjunctive (requiring any one criterion to disqualify).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief under 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1) only if they meet all three listed criminal-history criteria, or if possessing any one is disqualifying. This split leads to dramatically different sentences for defendants with identical criminal histories depending on the circuit.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required to disqualify
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or' in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law as a basis for compassionate release. Some circuits hold that such changes can never be extraordinary and compelling reasons, while others (and the Sentencing Commission's 2023 amendment at § 1B1.13(b)(6)) permit consideration of non-retroactive changes producing unusually long sentences. This directly affects thousands of federal prisoners serving stacked § 924(c) sentences.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as one factor among others in evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons exist for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state criminalizes a substance not listed on the federal Controlled Substances Act schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the underlying substance to be federally scheduled, or whether any state-law drug offense qualifies regardless of federal scheduling. This issue directly affects base offense levels and career-offender designations for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: State offense qualifies regardless of federal scheduling
positions.summary: Holds that 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines broadly includes any state drug offense, even if the substance is not controlled under federal law, because there is no textual basis to graft a federal-law limitation onto the guideline.

positions.label: State offense must involve a federally scheduled substance
positions.summary: Holds that a prior state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the substance at issue is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to an appeal from the denial of a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment.
splits.description: Circuits have split on whether a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (subject to the 30-day civil appeal deadline) or part of the criminal case itself (subject to the 14-day criminal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, and therefore the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment fee motion is part of the criminal case, and the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held it belongs to the estate, creating a split with circuits that read § 348(f)(1)(A) as preserving the debtor's interest in post-petition appreciation.

positions.label: Post-petition appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that under § 348(f)(1)(A) and § 541(a), post-petition appreciation in property value inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Post-petition appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A) limits the converted estate to property as valued at the time of the original petition filing, so post-petition appreciation in equity belongs to the debtor, not the estate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may expressly consider punishment of the defendant as a factor when revoking supervised release and imposing a revocation sentence.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether punishment is a permissible purpose of a revocation sentence upon violation of supervised release. The Sixth Circuit (under United States v. Lewis) permits district courts to expressly punish defendants for violations, placing it at the extreme of a circuit split, while other circuits hold that supervised release revocation sentences should focus on the violation of trust and deterrence rather than retribution.

positions.label: Punishment is a permissible purpose
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly consider punishment as a factor when imposing a sentence upon revocation of supervised release for a violation.

positions.label: Punishment is not the primary purpose
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences should focus on the breach of trust inherent in the violation and deterrence, rather than punishment for the underlying conduct, and that express reliance on punishment is error.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that orders denying motions to seal medical records are immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of 'high order' that would be effectively unreviewable after final judgment. Other circuits have not uniformly recognized this category of collateral order, creating potential disagreement on the scope of the doctrine.

positions.label: Immediately appealable as collateral order
positions.summary: Holds that denials of motions to seal medical information satisfy the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of high order that cannot be adequately vindicated after final judgment.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal are generally not immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because they can be remedied post-judgment or through other mechanisms like mandamus.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which prohibits states from enacting laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any motor carrier ... with respect to the transportation of property,' extends to preempt common-law negligence claims against freight brokers for injuries caused by carriers they selected.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted by the FAAAA because they are 'related to' the broker's service of arranging transportation.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that common-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent hiring of carriers are not preempted because they are too tenuously connected to broker 'services' to fall within the FAAAA's preemption clause.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether compassionate release motions may encompass arguments that overlap with claims typically raised in habeas petitions under § 2255, such as claims of legal error in the original conviction or sentence. Some circuits permit consideration of such factors while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) may not be used as a substitute for habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider legal errors or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: May not consider — § 3582 is not a habeas substitute
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) may not be used to challenge the validity of a conviction or sentence, as those claims must be raised through § 2255.
